title: NIS2 Cryptography Policy Compliance — TLS and Certificate Posture
exclusions: Does not operate hardware security modules, manage root key ceremonies, or decide which national algorithms are legally recognized—only organizational policy alignment and monitoring.
evidence: `UC-22.2.29` correlation workbook, redacted `stream:tls` pcap-to-SPL reconciliation notes, inventory CSV hashes, and RFC3161 signature bundle stored per `evidenceSigning` alongside PKI CAB drafts.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Uplifted from contributing: SPL directly addresses Art.21(2)(h) requirements with specific, actionable monitoring.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIS2 Art.21(2)(h) (Cryptography and encryption) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.2.29: NIS2 Cryptography Policy Compliance — TLS and Certificate Posture.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.2.29' running on TLS metadata from Stream, certificate inventory, nis2_crypto_policy.csv (min_tls_version, allowed_ciphers), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Staging VIP intentionally negotiates TLSv1.0 on port 443 while scoped map marks `nis2_in_scope=true`; within one hour the alert surfaces `weak_tls=1`, `service_name=payments_api`, proving enforcement targets in-scope listeners only.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Production uplift completes with TLSv1.3 only, new certificates carry ninety-day lifetimes managed via automated ACME, scoped map retires the lab IP, and seven consecutive runs return zero rows while external Qualys SSL scan shows grade A for the same endpoint.

dataSources: `index=pki` `sourcetype=stream:tls` (dest_ip, dest_port, tls_version, cipher, expiry_epoch) via Splunk Add-on for Stream ([Splunkbase 1809](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/1809)); `index=certificates` `sourcetype=certificate:inventory` for `ssl_serial`, `ssl_issuer`, `ssl_end_time` aligned to CIM Certificates; lookup `nis2_crypto_policy.csv` supplying `min_tls_version`, `allowed_ciphers`; optional Splunk Enterprise Security ([Splunkbase 263](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/263)) for service_name enrichment.
app: Splunk Add-on for Stream (Splunkbase 1809), Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (Splunkbase 1621)
spl:
index=pki sourcetype="stream:tls" earliest=-24h
| stats latest(tls_version) as tls_version latest(cipher) as cipher values(expiry_epoch) as cert_expiry by dest_ip dest_port
| lookup nis2_scoped_service_map dest_ip OUTPUT service_name nis2_in_scope
| where nis2_in_scope="true"
| eval weak_tls=if(match(tls_version,"SSLv|TLSv1\.0|TLSv1\.1"),1,0)
| eval cert_problem=if(cert_expiry < relative_time(now(),"+30d@d"),1,0)
| where weak_tls=1 OR cert_problem=1
| table service_name, dest_ip, dest_port, tls_version, cipher, cert_problem
description: Correlates passive TLS observations from Stream with inventory-derived certificate expirations and `nis2_scoped_service_map` to list only NIS2-scoped listeners that run outdated protocols or face thirty-day renewals.
value: Weak TLS versions are invisible until a pen test or outage; combining wire-derived protocol data with scoped service context lets risk owners prioritize renewals that actually map to regulated workloads instead of every lab VM in the enterprise.
implementation: (1) Mirror production VLANs to Stream. (2) Maintain `nis2_scoped_service_map`. (3) Import `nis2_crypto_policy.csv`. (4) Alert on `weak_tls=1`. (5) PKI workflow webhook on `cert_problem=1`. (6) Annual evidence export + `evidenceSigning`.
visualization: Table with color rules on tls_version, timeline for cert_expiry, map of dest_ip.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t min(All_Certificates.ssl_end_time) as soonest from datamodel=Certificates.All_Certificates by All_Certificates.ssl_subject | where soonest < relative_time(now(),"+30d")
dataModelAcceleration: Accelerate Certificates if inventory model used heavily
knownFalsePositives: 1) **Legacy mainframe TN3270 TLS gateways** cannot upgrade beyond TLSv1.1 until vendor patch. *Distinguish:* `service_name` matches `nis2_crypto_policy.csv` exception list. *Suppress:* `lookup nis2_crypto_policy service_name OUTPUT waive_until`. 2) **Stream misses TLS 1.3 downgrade to plaintext** because capture ACL changed. *Distinguish:* event volume drop >50%. *Suppress:* do not suppress—open infra ticket; only pause alert via time-bound exception register. 3) **Shared load balancer IP reused** mapping wrong `service_name`. *Distinguish:* `dest_port` differing entries. *Suppress:* enrich via `lookup vip_service dest_ip dest_port`. 4) **Certificate inventory stale** triggers false cert_problem. *Distinguish:* `certificate:inventory _indextime` older than 7d. *Suppress:* join latest inventory `| join ...` with freshness filter. 5) **Pentest TLS downgrade tooling** during sanctioned test week. *Distinguish:* `src_ip` in `pentest_supernet`. *Suppress:* `where NOT cidrmatch("192.0.2.0/24",src_ip)` assuming Stream forwards client IP.
equipmentModels: Qualys SSL Labs / Tenable.io (external TLS posture validation)
Splunk Add-on for Stream (Splunkbase 1809, stream:tls field extractions)
Splunk Enterprise Security 7.x (asset enrichment, service scoping)
Splunk Stream Independent Forwarder (passive TLS handshake capture)
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Splunk Stream Independent Forwarder (ISF) ≥8.0 deployed on the same capture infrastructure as UC-22.2.11. This UC reuses the same `index=pki sourcetype=stream:tls` data stream, but applies a different analysis: UC-22.2.11 focuses on certificate expiry; this UC focuses on TLS protocol version and cipher compliance for NIS2-scoped services specifically.
• Splunk Add-on for Stream (Splunkbase 1809) ≥8.0 on Search Heads and Indexers for `stream:tls` field extractions. The key fields for this UC are: `tls_version` (e.g., `TLSv1.0`, `TLSv1.2`, `TLSv1.3`), `cipher` or `cipher_suite` (e.g., `TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384`, `TLS_RSA_WITH_RC4_128_SHA`), `dest_ip`, `dest_port`, `ssl_subject_common_name` (from the server certificate), `server_name` (from the ClientHello SNI extension).
• Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263) ≥7.0 optional for asset enrichment — ES maps `dest_ip` to `service_name` via the `assets_by_cidr` or `assets_by_str` lookups. Without ES, you need to maintain the `nis2_scoped_service_map` lookup manually.
• CSV `nis2_scoped_service_map.csv` with columns: `dest_ip` (IP address of the service), `service_name` (human-readable name, e.g., `payments_api`, `hr_portal`, `scada_gateway`), `nis2_in_scope` (`true`/`false` — whether this service falls under NIS2 obligations), `owner_team` (team responsible for TLS configuration), `last_reviewed` (ISO 8601). This CSV is the scoping mechanism that focuses the alert on NIS2-relevant services only — without it, the search would fire for every dev VM running TLS 1.0, which is noise.
• CSV `nis2_crypto_policy.csv` with columns: `service_family` (e.g., `web`, `api`, `database`, `legacy`), `min_tls_version` (`TLSv1.2` or `TLSv1.3`), `allowed_cipher_suites` (comma-separated list of acceptable cipher suites, e.g., `TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384,TLS_CHACHA20_POLY1305_SHA256,TLS_AES_128_GCM_SHA256`), `disallowed_ciphers` (explicit blocklist: `RC4,DES,3DES,MD5,NULL,EXPORT`). This CSV encodes the organisation's cryptographic policy derived from ENISA recommendations and internal security standards.
• Index `pki` with retention ≥ 90 days (same as UC-22.2.11). Index `certificates` for inventory scans. Index `audit_evidence` for compliance output.
• RBAC: role `pki_analyst` with `srchIndexesAllowed = pki, certificates, audit_evidence`.
• SPAN port / mirror configuration: same as UC-22.2.11. The capture must include the network segments carrying NIS2-scoped service traffic. If NIS2-scoped services are in a separate VLAN (recommended), mirror that VLAN specifically.
• Baseline knowledge: TLS version compliance ladder — SSLv2/SSLv3: broken (POODLE), TLS 1.0: deprecated (BEAST, CRIME), TLS 1.1: deprecated (no known practical attacks but weak cipher support), TLS 1.2: acceptable (with AEAD ciphers like AES-GCM), TLS 1.3: preferred (simplified handshake, mandatory forward secrecy, no legacy cipher negotiation). ENISA and BSI (German Federal Office for Information Security) guidance requires TLS 1.2+ for NIS2 compliance.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
This UC relies on the same data pipeline as UC-22.2.11 (`index=pki sourcetype=stream:tls`). If UC-22.2.11 is already deployed, no additional data collection is needed.

**Service scoping map deployment:**
Import `nis2_scoped_service_map.csv` to `etc/apps/SA-NIS2/lookups/` via Deployment Server. This is the critical differentiator between UC-22.2.11 (which monitors ALL certificates) and this UC (which monitors only NIS2-scoped services).

Validation of the scoping map:
```spl
index=pki sourcetype=stream:tls earliest=-24h dest_ip=*
| stats count dc(ssl_subject_common_name) as certs by dest_ip dest_port
| lookup nis2_scoped_service_map.csv dest_ip OUTPUT service_name nis2_in_scope
| eval scope=if(nis2_in_scope="true", "NIS2-SCOPED", if(isnull(nis2_in_scope), "UNCLASSIFIED", "NOT-SCOPED"))
| stats sum(count) as events sum(certs) as certificates by scope
```

This shows how many events and certificates fall into each scoping category. If `UNCLASSIFIED` is large, the scoping map is incomplete — investigate those IPs and classify them. For a mature deployment, `UNCLASSIFIED` should be < 5% of total events.

**Crypto policy deployment:**
Import `nis2_crypto_policy.csv` alongside the scoping map. Link service families: `| lookup nis2_scoped_service_map.csv dest_ip OUTPUT service_name service_family | lookup nis2_crypto_policy.csv service_family OUTPUT min_tls_version allowed_cipher_suites`.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Save as `UC-22.2.29 NIS2 TLS Compliance`.

```spl
index=pki sourcetype=stream:tls earliest=-24h
| stats latest(tls_version) as tls_version latest(cipher) as cipher values(expiry_epoch) as cert_expiry by dest_ip dest_port
| lookup nis2_scoped_service_map dest_ip OUTPUT service_name nis2_in_scope
| where nis2_in_scope="true"
| eval weak_tls=if(match(tls_version, "SSLv|TLSv1\.0|TLSv1\.1"), 1, 0)
| eval cert_problem=if(cert_expiry < relative_time(now(), "+30d@d"), 1, 0)
| where weak_tls=1 OR cert_problem=1
| table service_name, dest_ip, dest_port, tls_version, cipher, cert_problem
```

Understanding this SPL — design decisions:

• **`latest(tls_version)` per dest_ip/port pair**: takes the most recently observed TLS negotiation for each service endpoint. If a server is upgraded mid-day from TLS 1.1 to TLS 1.2, the `latest()` shows the current state (TLS 1.2), not the historical state. This is intentional — the search detects current posture, not historical violations.

• **`values(expiry_epoch)` as `cert_expiry`**: certificates may have multiple expiry values if the certificate chain is observed (leaf, intermediate, root). The `values()` function captures all of them. The `cert_problem` eval uses `< relative_time(now(), "+30d@d")` which fires if ANY certificate in the chain expires within 30 days. For most organisations, the leaf certificate is the one that matters — use `min(expiry_epoch)` instead of `values()` for cleaner single-value comparison.

• **NIS2 scoping via lookup**: the lookup join is the key differentiator. Without it, this search is a generic TLS compliance monitor. With it, it becomes a NIS2-specific control — only services classified as `nis2_in_scope=true` are monitored. This reduces noise dramatically in large environments where hundreds of internal services may legitimately use older TLS versions for backward compatibility.

• **Dual detection (weak_tls + cert_problem)**: combines protocol compliance and certificate freshness in one search. A service with TLS 1.3 but an expiring certificate is still a finding. A service with a valid certificate but TLS 1.0 is also a finding. This comprehensive approach maps to Art.21(2)(h) which covers both "cryptography" (protocol) and "encryption" (certificate/key management).

Extended search with cipher policy validation:
```spl
index=pki sourcetype=stream:tls earliest=-24h
| stats latest(tls_version) as tls_version latest(cipher) as cipher by dest_ip dest_port
| lookup nis2_scoped_service_map dest_ip OUTPUT service_name nis2_in_scope service_family
| where nis2_in_scope="true"
| lookup nis2_crypto_policy service_family OUTPUT min_tls_version allowed_cipher_suites disallowed_ciphers
| eval weak_tls=if(match(tls_version, "SSLv|TLSv1\.0|TLSv1\.1"), 1, 0)
| eval bad_cipher=if(match(cipher, "RC4|DES|3DES|MD5|NULL|EXPORT"), 1, 0)
| eval policy_compliant=if(weak_tls=0 AND bad_cipher=0, 1, 0)
| where policy_compliant=0
| table service_name, dest_ip, dest_port, tls_version, cipher, weak_tls, bad_cipher
```

This adds cipher-suite validation — even TLS 1.2 connections using RC4 or 3DES ciphers are non-compliant.

Schedule: cron `0 * * * *` (hourly). Time range: `-24h to now`. Trigger: `Number of results > 0`. Throttle by `dest_ip + dest_port` for 24 hours.

Alert actions: (1) For `weak_tls=1` on NIS2-scoped services: ES notable with `urgency=high`. (2) For `cert_problem=1`: email PKI operations. (3) For `bad_cipher=1`: email security engineering. (4) Write to `index=audit_evidence`.

Step 3 — Validate
(a) **TLS version verification**: pick an NIS2-scoped service from the results. From the command line: `openssl s_client -connect <dest_ip>:<dest_port> -servername <service> -tls1_2 2>&1 | grep "Protocol"`. Confirm the protocol matches what Splunk observed. If Splunk shows TLS 1.0 but `openssl` shows TLS 1.2, a middle-box may be intercepting and downgrading.

(b) **Cipher verification**: `openssl s_client -connect <dest_ip>:<dest_port> -servername <service> 2>&1 | grep "Cipher is"`. Confirm the cipher matches Splunk's `cipher` field. Note: Stream captures the negotiated cipher, not the server's full cipher list. The server may support stronger ciphers but the client requested a weaker one.

(c) **Scoping accuracy**: review the `nis2_scoped_service_map.csv` against the current network asset inventory. New services deployed since the last CSV update may not be classified. Run the unclassified check from Step 1 monthly.

(d) **External validation with Qualys SSL Labs**: for internet-facing NIS2-scoped services, run an SSL Labs scan (ssllabs.com/ssltest) and compare the grade to Splunk's findings. SSL Labs provides an independent assessment of TLS configuration quality. Grade A = TLS 1.2+/1.3 with strong ciphers; Grade C or below = compliance concern.

(e) **Negative test**: verify that services with `nis2_in_scope=false` do NOT appear in the alert output, even if they use TLS 1.0. This confirms the scoping filter is working correctly.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Dashboard (recommended name: `NIS2 TLS Compliance Posture`):
• **Row 1 — Headline tiles**: Single-value `NIS2-scoped services with weak TLS` (red ≥ 1). Single-value `NIS2-scoped services with expiring certs` (amber ≥ 1). Single-value `Services with blocked ciphers` (red ≥ 1). Single-value `Total NIS2-scoped services monitored`.
• **Row 2 — TLS version distribution**: pie chart of `tls_version` for NIS2-scoped services. Ideal: 100% TLS 1.2/1.3. Any TLS 1.0/1.1 slice is a compliance gap.
• **Row 3 — Non-compliant services table**: sortable by service_name, dest_ip, dest_port, tls_version, cipher, weak_tls, bad_cipher, cert_problem. Drilldown shows the full TLS negotiation history for the selected service.
• **Row 4 — 7-day compliance trend**: timechart of non-compliant service count over 7 days. Step-function drops indicate successful remediation; step-function rises indicate new non-compliant services.

Runbook (owner: Security Engineering):
1. Hourly: review weak TLS alerts. For NIS2-scoped services using TLS 1.0/1.1: (a) Check if a deprecation plan exists. If yes, verify the timeline. If no, create a ServiceNow CHG ticket with the service owner. (b) If the service cannot be upgraded (legacy vendor constraint), document an exception in `nis2_crypto_exception_register.csv` with compensating controls (WAF fronting, network segmentation, monitoring intensification).
2. For cipher violations: contact the server administrator. Most cipher issues are configuration problems — the server supports strong ciphers but also advertises weak ones. Fix: update the server's cipher configuration to disable RC4/DES/3DES/NULL/EXPORT.
3. Monthly: present TLS compliance trend to the CISO. Correlate with Qualys SSL Labs external scan results for internet-facing services.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting

• **`dest_ip` is null on Stream events.** NAT metadata is not being captured. Stream needs to see the actual IP, not the NAT'd address. If the ISF is behind a NAT gateway, the `dest_ip` field shows the NAT IP, not the server IP. Fix: place the ISF on a SPAN port that mirrors pre-NAT traffic (between the server and the load balancer, not between the load balancer and the client).

• **`cert_expiry` is multivalue, causing `cert_problem` to be unreliable.** When Stream captures a full certificate chain (leaf + intermediate + root), `expiry_epoch` has multiple values. The root CA certificate may have a very long expiry (20+ years) while the leaf certificate is expiring in 10 days. Fix: use `min(expiry_epoch)` instead of `values(expiry_epoch)` in the stats command to capture only the soonest-expiring certificate in the chain.

• **High false-positive rate for `weak_tls=1`.** Middle-boxes (SSL inspection proxies like Blue Coat, Zscaler, Palo Alto decryption) terminate TLS from the client and re-encrypt to the server with a different TLS version. Stream sees the middle-box-to-server leg, which may use a weaker TLS version than the client-to-middle-box leg. Fix: mirror the client-facing segment to get the true client-negotiated TLS version. Or accept the middle-box leg as the actual server posture — if the server itself only supports TLS 1.0, that IS the compliance finding regardless of the middle-box.

• **Scoping lookup stale after cloud migration.** IP addresses changed during a cloud migration (on-premises to AWS/Azure), but `nis2_scoped_service_map.csv` still has the old IPs. New cloud service IPs are unclassified. Fix: automate the scoping map refresh from the CMDB or cloud provider API. For AWS: `aws ec2 describe-instances --filters "Name=tag:nis2_scope,Values=true"`. For Azure: query Azure Resource Graph.

• **Stream drops TLS 1.3 handshakes.** Older versions of Stream (< 8.0) cannot parse TLS 1.3 handshakes because the message format changed significantly (encrypted extensions, 0-RTT). The ISF silently drops TLS 1.3 connections, making them invisible. Fix: upgrade Stream to ≥8.0 which adds TLS 1.3 parsing support. Verify with `index=pki sourcetype=stream:tls tls_version="TLSv1.3" earliest=-1h | stats count` — if zero but you know TLS 1.3 services exist, Stream is not parsing them.

• **Service appears compliant in Splunk but fails Qualys SSL Labs scan.** Possible causes: (a) Splunk sees only one TLS negotiation variant (the one used by the most common client), while SSL Labs tests all supported protocol versions. The server may negotiate TLS 1.2 with most clients but still offer TLS 1.0 as a fallback. (b) SSL Labs tests cipher preference, certificate chain completeness, and other aspects beyond what Stream captures. Fix: use SSL Labs as the authoritative external validation; Splunk provides continuous internal monitoring between external scans.
grandmaExplanation: We listen quietly to how computers greet each other on the network and check whether they use strong secret handshakes. If a handshake is too old or a security badge is about to run out of date, we flag only the systems the law cares about most.
